summary: This post, written by the Alternative Banking group of Occupy Wall Street, catalogues the worst Wall Street abuses of 2013 and highlights grassroots victories against financial industry power. The authors document how banks manipulated commodities markets, evaded prosecution, exploited homeowners through 'zombie foreclosures,' influenced legislation, and pushed for trade deals that would constrain regulation. They also note silver linings including Occupy the SEC's influence on the Volcker Rule, Richmond's creative use of eminent domain to help underwater homeowners, and Larry Summers not becoming Fed Chair. The piece argues that Wall Street's grip on Washington remains strong, but citizen activism and investigative journalism are fighting back.
key_points: Citigroup literally wrote legislation (HR 992) to roll back derivatives restrictions, with language adopted nearly word-for-word by Congress
Despite JP Morgan setting aside $28 billion for legal fees, no criminal prosecutions occurred—Eric Holder admitted megabanks were 'too big to prosecute'
Banks exploited homeowners through 'zombie foreclosures' where they evicted owners but didn't file papers, continuing to rack up fees on people who thought they'd lost their homes
Occupy the SEC's 325-page comment letter was cited 284 times in the final Volcker Rule, demonstrating grassroots impact on financial regulation
AIG's CEO compared criticism of executive bonuses to Jim Crow lynchings, revealing Wall Street's cultural disconnect from accountability
money_quotes: Mistaking a papercut for a crucifixion is the inevitable delusion of a pampered and under-prosecuted criminal upper class.
If only more cases had been brought to trial: juries are chomping at the bit to find banks guilty. Which is probably why more weren't.

Holder also admitted to Congress in March that the megabanks were too big to prosecute. While he later retracted the statement, his actions — never bringing criminal charges despite admitted criminal activities — speak louder than his words.
Minimal fines the banks have paid are simply the cost of doing business and are far outweighed by the money they've made.
When JPMorgan Chase wanted to settle their many legal difficulties, CEO Jamie Dimon had a private meeting with Holder to negotiate.

Larry Summers may finally have made too many obvious and ridiculous mistakes — deregulating financial derivatives to pave the way for the financial crisis, for one, and aiding criminal fraud in Russia for another — to be made, once again, one of the most powerful people in the world.
key_insight: Wall Street's worst abuses persist not despite government oversight but because of regulatory capture, yet grassroots movements like Occupy the SEC prove that organized citizen engagement can still influence the rules.
